content.problemStatement:
Your best friend has a very interesting necklace with nnn pearls. On each of the pearls of the necklace there is an integer. However, your friend wants to modify the necklace a bit and asks you for help. She wants to move the first pearl kkk spots to the left (and do so with all other pearls).

For example: if the necklace was originally 1,5,3,4,21, 5, 3, 4, 21,5,3,4,2 and k=2k = 2k=2, now it becomes 3,4,2,1,53, 4, 2, 1, 53,4,2,1,5.

Help your best friend determine how the necklace will look after the modification.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input, the first containing two integers n,kn, kn,k.
The second line of each test case contains nnn integers a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2, ..., a_na1​,a2​,...,an​ representing the integers on the pearls starting from the first one.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line nnn integers representing the necklace after modification.
constraints: The sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers n and k (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5, 0 ≤ k ≤ n). The second line contains n integers a1, a2, ..., an representing the values on the pearls.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with n integers – the order of the pearls after rotating the necklace left by k positions.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case rotates left by 3: (1,5,3) move to the end → 4 2 1 5 3. Second test case rotates left by 5: (10,1,2,9,8) move to the end → 2 10 1 2 9 8.
A single element never changes regardless of k.
Rotate left by 2: first two elements (7,-3) go to the end.

solution.approach: For each test case we need to move the first k elements to the end while preserving their order. Because rotating left by n is the same as no rotation, we first replace k by k % n (if n > 0). The resulting sequence is arr[k], arr[k+1], ..., arr[n-1], arr[0], arr[1], ..., arr[k-1]. This can be produced in O(n) time by iterating once over the array and printing the element at index (k + i) % n for i = 0 … n-1. The algorithm uses O(1) additional memory besides the input array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, overall O(total n) ≤ O(3·10^5)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing the input array of each test case (the algorithm itself uses O(1) extra space)

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        n = int(next(it))
        k = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        if n:
            k %= n
        rotated = arr[k:] + arr[:k]
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, rotated)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
